sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 21, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Maze navigation puzzle. Player is a black dot at (10,16) inside a blue 5x5 start region. Target is a blue region at bottom-right with a blue dot. White corridors are walkable paths, black (B) are walls. Red elements may be obstacles or path markers. Blue bar at row 63 is likely a HUD timer.
- Goal model: Navigate from start (top-left blue region) to target (bottom-right blue region) through white corridors.
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player through the maze.
- Plan: First probe with RIGHT to confirm movement mechanics, then solve the maze path.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 20 of about 78 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The red area has changed! Let me compare:
- Before (when player was at start): R boundary had 20 points, pixels=82
- Now (player at cols 38-42): R boundary=[[13, 40], [40, 40], [40, 25], [41, 24], [42, 24], [42, 20], [41, 21], [40, 20], [39, 21], [38, 20], [38, 24], [39, 24], [40, 25], [40, 39], [39, 40]], pixels=66
The red area now has 66 pixels (down from 82). The boundary has changed:
- Before: [[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18], [42, 14], [41, 15], [40, 14], [39, 15], [38, 14], [38, 18], [39, 18], [40, 19], [40, 39], [39, 40], [12, 40], [11, 39]]
- Now: [[13, 40], [40, 40], [40, 25], [41, 24], [42, 24], [42, 20], [41, 21], [40, 20], [39, 21], [38, 20], [38, 24], [39, 24], [40, 25], [40, 39], [39, 40]]
The red area has shifted! Before it was at col 40 (vertical) and row 40 (horizontal) with a loop at cols 14-18. Now it's at col 40 (vertical from row 13 to 40) and row 40 (horizontal from col 20 to 40) with a loop at cols 20-24.
So when the player moved RIGHT 4 times, the red area also moved! The red area seems to follow the player's horizontal position.
Wait, let me think about this more carefully. The red area before was:
- Vertical at col 40, rows 9-40
- Horizontal at row 40, cols 14-40
- Loop at cols 14-18, rows 38-42
The red area now is:
- Vertical at col 40, rows 13-40
- Horizontal at row 40, cols 20-40
- Loop at cols 20-24, rows 38-42
The red area shifted right by 6 columns (from cols 14-18 to cols 20-24). This matches the player's movement! The player moved RIGHT 4 times, each time moving 6 columns, so 4*6=24 columns. But the red area only shifted by 6 columns... wait, that doesn't match.
Actually, let me re-examine. The player started at cols 14-18 and moved to cols 38-42. That's a shift of 24 columns. But the red area's loop shifted from cols 14-18 to cols 20-24, which is a shift of 6 columns.
Hmm, maybe the red area is not following the player's position, but rather the red area is being "pushed" by the player. When the player moves RIGHT, the red area moves RIGHT as well, but only by a certain amount.
Wait, let me look at the red area more carefully. The red area before had a vertical segment at col 40 from row 9 to row 40. Now it has a vertical segment at col 40 from row 13 to row 40. The top of the vertical segment moved from row 9 to row 13, which is 4 rows down.
Actually, I think the red area is a "rope" that connects the player to the bottom of the maze. When the player moves RIGHT, the rope stretches and changes shape. When the player presses SPACE, the rope retracts and the player returns to start.
But wait, when I pressed SPACE earlier, the red area remained at 82 pixels. So the red area doesn't retract when the player presses SPACE.
Let me think about this differently. The red area seems to be a path that the player needs to follow. The player needs to move along the red path to reach the target.
Looking at the current red area:
- Vertical at col 40, rows 13-40
- Horizontal at row 40, cols 20-40
- Loop at cols 20-24, rows 38-42
The player is at cols 38-42, rows 8-12. The player is adjacent to the red area at col 40.
Now, the player can't move DOWN from the current position (only 1 cell changed, which is the HUD). So the player is still trapped in the blue region.
Wait, but the red area has changed. Maybe the red area is a "key" or "token" that the player needs to collect. When the player moves to a position adjacent to the red area, the red area changes.
Let me try pressing SPACE now to see what happens. Maybe SPACE does something different when the red area has changed.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the red area more carefully. The red area before had 82 pixels, and now it has 66 pixels. The red area has shrunk! Maybe the player "consumed" part of the red area by moving adjacent to it.
Let me try pressing SPACE to see if the player returns to start and the red area resets.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: Red area changed from 82 to 66 pixels when player moved RIGHT 4 times. Red area shifted right (loop from cols 14-18 to 20-24). Player at cols 38-42, still can't move DOWN. Need to understand red area mechanics.
